title: The Black Compass Route
subtitle: Trace the cargo before the river swallows the route.
description: A pirate adventure in supply chains and trade
cover_caption:
THE BLACK COMPASS ROUTE

Trace the cargo before the river swallows the route.

A pirate adventure in supply chains and trade
route_summary: Vorobyovy Gory → Cable Car viewpoint → Andreevskaya Embankment → Andreevsky Bridge → Neskuchny Garden → Pushkinskaya Embankment → Golitsyn Pond → Krymskaya Embankment → Monument to Peter I
start_message:
THE BLACK COMPASS ROUTE

Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Latitude: 55°42'N | Longitude: 37°33'E

The Logbook has opened by its own cursed will.

Its pages smell of salt, tar and old stormwater, though no sea lies anywhere near this hill. The ink crawls across the parchment as if some drowned hand still writes from beneath the tide, and in the corner of the first page burns the mark of the Black Compass — a compass without a north point.

Crew, ye have found the journal of Captain Mordecai Flint, the Caribbean smuggler who never wasted his talents stealing ordinary treasure. Gold can be counted, jewels can be sold, but a stolen route can make any cargo look innocent.

Flint’s final prize was called The Cargo of Eternity: a perfect supply chain that could move goods across the world without delay, loss, unpaid tariff, false supplier, broken warehouse or cursed bottleneck.

Yet Flint trusted no scallywag with such power, so he scattered the secret along a river course in Moscow, a city with no sea but more than enough water, stone and shadow to hide a pirate’s map.

To claim the Cargo, ye must survive ten trials and restore ten lost words of trade.

Type START if ye dare to open the chart.
rules_message:
Ship’s Articles // Terms of Safe Passage

Before the Tidebound Crew may set sail, the Logbook demands an oath of careful conduct.

Keep to public ground. Enter no restricted place. Trouble no workers, visitors, guards or honest townsfolk. Do not run near water, climb structures, block pathways or cross roads as if chased by the Kraken himself.

This voyage is fictional, though the lessons of trade be true. Read what is visible. Observe what is public. Reason as a crew.

A reckless pirate finds the deep. A careful crew brings the cargo home.

Type I AGREE to sign the Ship’s Articles.

stages.obstacle: The Wind of the Broken Ledger
stages.message:
Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Latitude: 55°42'N | Longitude: 37°33'E
Entry I: The Crow’s Nest of Moscow

Avast, crew, for our voyage begins not at dockside among barrels and ropes, but high above the river, where any worthy lookout would plant his boots, narrow his eyes against the wind and read the city as if it were a sea chart.

From this height Moscow appears calm, yet the Logbook trembles in my hands, and a violent wind rises from nowhere to tear at its pages. This is no honest weather. It is the Wind of the Broken Ledger, a cursed gust that scatters records, confuses counts and turns a well-supplied ship into a floating argument.

A captain who cannot account for what lies in the hold is defeated before the anchor is raised. He may have courage, a sharp blade and a crew willing to sing through a storm, but if he cannot say how many barrels, bolts, crates, tools and rations are aboard, then the voyage belongs not to him, but to chance.

TASK FOR THE CREW:
What one-word term means the stock of goods that a business counts, keeps and manages before sale or shipment?
hints: A quartermaster checks this before a long voyage.
It is what a business has “in stock”.
stages.wrong_reply: The Wind of the Broken Ledger howls louder, and the page refuses your mark. Ye have named something aboard the ship, perhaps, but not the counted stock inside the hold.
stages.success_reply:
The Wind of the Broken Ledger weakens, and the scattered pages return to the Logbook like gulls circling back to a mast after the storm has passed.

Recovered word: INVENTORY.

The hold is counted, the first lock has opened, and the voyage may begin; yet the river below darkens with movement, and the next trial waits where time itself hangs above the water like a cursed hourglass.
stages.next_location: Proceed to the Upper Cable Car Station / viewpoint area. You do not need to ride the cable car; use the view and the route as your clue.

stages.location_name: Upper Cable Car Station / Viewpoint Area
stages.address_hint: Viewpoint area near the Moscow cable car, Vorobyovy Gory side
stages.message:
Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Latitude: 55°42'N | Longitude: 37°33'E
Entry II: The Hourglass in the Sky

Above the river, the cables stretch like rigging from an invisible ship, and every carriage that crosses the air seems to mock the crew with the same cruel lesson: movement is never instant, and every promise of arrival must pass through time.

A cursed hourglass swings somewhere beyond sight, and with each grain that falls the voyage grows heavier. Orders are placed, but goods do not arrive. Merchants swear the cargo is coming, customers grow restless, and the captain learns that delay can sink trust as surely as a cannonball sinks wood.

Captain Flint once wrote that distance did not ruin merchants half so often as waiting did, for a cargo delayed too long may arrive technically correct and commercially useless.

TASK FOR THE CREW:
What supply-chain term means the time between placing an order and receiving it?

Send it as one word: LEADTIME.
hints: It measures waiting, not distance.
It begins when the order is placed and ends when the goods arrive.
stages.wrong_reply: The cursed hourglass turns again, and more sand falls into the sea. Ye have measured movement, perhaps, but not the full waiting time between order and arrival.
stages.success_reply:
The cursed hourglass cracks, and its sand spills upward into the wind as if time itself has remembered how to sail.

Recovered word: LEADTIME.

The delay is named, and therefore it can be managed. Make for the riverbank now, for ghost barges have been sighted in the mist, and their holds ride low with commercial cargo.
stages.next_location: Go down toward Andreevskaya Embankment and find the river path.

stages.address_hint: Andreevskaya Embankment, along the Moscow River
stages.message:
Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Latitude: 55°42'N | Longitude: 37°33'E
Entry III: The Cargo on Black Water

The river appears below the path, wide and silver under the uncertain sky, carrying reflections the way old trade routes carry secrets.

Out of the mist come barges so low in the water that they seem to carry not passengers, but every unpaid debt the river has ever refused to drown. No sailor waves from the deck. No lantern swings in welcome. These are merchant vessels of the damned, burdened with crates, barrels and goods whose stories were erased before they reached the quay.

A passenger carries luggage, but a merchant moves something larger, heavier and more accountable. To misunderstand that difference is to let the ghost fleet board us.

TASK FOR THE CREW:
What one-word term means goods transported in bulk by ship, road, rail or air?
hints: You may know phrases such as “air _____” or “rail _____”.
It is commercial cargo, not a traveller’s suitcase.
stages.wrong_reply: The ghost barges draw closer, their holds groaning in the dark. Ye have named a vehicle or journey, perhaps, but not the commercial goods being carried.
stages.success_reply:
The ghost barges drift back into fog, their black sails folding into the mist like secrets returned to the deep.

Recovered word: FREIGHT.

The cargo is named, but naming it will not save us long. Ahead lies a narrow crossing, and any pirate who has survived a reef knows that one blocked passage can trap an entire fleet.
stages.next_location: Continue toward Andreevsky / Pushkinsky Pedestrian Bridge.

stages.address_hint: Pedestrian bridge across the Moscow River between Frunzenskaya and Pushkinskaya embankments
stages.message:
Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Latitude: 55°42'N | Longitude: 37°34'E
Entry IV: The Narrow Passage

We have reached a crossing where the city narrows itself into a single path, and the river below watches like a patient beast waiting for one careless step.

No pirate fears the open sea half so much as a narrow channel. In open water a ship may turn, flee, fight or vanish into weather, but in a chokepoint every vessel must wait its turn, and the fastest ship becomes no faster than the slowest passage.

So it is with supply chains. One overloaded port, one missing document, one slow supplier, one broken crane or one cursed customs desk can make the whole route groan like a mast under storm wind.

TASK FOR THE CREW:
What one-word term means the point in a process that slows down the entire flow because capacity is limited?
hints: Picture rum trying to leave a bottle through a narrow neck.
It is the slowest point that limits the whole system.
stages.wrong_reply: The ambushers remain in the narrow passage, blades drawn. Ye have named delay, perhaps, but not the constricted point that causes the delay.
stages.success_reply:
The ambushers of the narrow passage scatter, and the bridge no longer feels like a trap set by the Kraken’s own quartermaster.

Recovered word: BOTTLENECK.

The crossing is clear, though the Logbook warns that no voyage survives on movement alone; somewhere ahead, the crew must learn how supplies are chosen before they ever reach the deck.
stages.next_location: Enter the Neskuchny Garden side and head toward the Hunting Lodge area.

stages.address_hint: Hunting Lodge area in Neskuchny Garden
stages.message:
Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Latitude: 55°43'N | Longitude: 37°35'E
Entry V: The Quartermaster’s Bargain

The path slips into older greenery, where the city softens its voice and even the wind seems to walk more carefully between the trees.

Here waits the ghost of a quartermaster with a ledger chained to his wrist and a face as pale as sailcloth left too long in moonlight. He blocks the crew’s passage not with a blade, but with a question older than piracy itself: how shall a ship sail if no one has obtained what the voyage requires?

Rope, powder, biscuit, canvas, lamp oil, spare timber, medicine, tools and water do not appear because a captain shouts for them. They must be sourced, selected, purchased and secured before the voyage begins.

TASK FOR THE CREW:
What one-word term means the organised process of obtaining goods or services for an organisation?
hints: It is more formal than buying.
It includes choosing where goods come from and obtaining them properly.
stages.wrong_reply: The quartermaster’s chain tightens around his ledger. Ye have named buying, perhaps, but not the organised business process that secures what is needed.
stages.success_reply:
The cursed quartermaster bows, and the chain around his ledger falls away with the weary sound of a debt finally paid.

Recovered word: PROCUREMENT.

The supplies are secured, yet the Logbook’s ink darkens at the edge of the page; for a ship may obtain goods honestly or foolishly, depending on the character of the one who provides them.
stages.next_location: Continue through Neskuchny Garden paths. The False Merchant waits somewhere among the trees.

stages.message:
Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Latitude: 55°43'N | Longitude: 37°35'E
Entry VI: The False Merchant of the Garden

A merchant waits among the trees, smiling with the polished ease of a man who has sold the same lie in twelve ports and been thanked for it in eleven.

His crates are clean, his seals are fresh, and his promises are sweeter than Caribbean rum. Yet Captain Flint warned every clever pirate that goods are only as trustworthy as the party standing behind them, and a bargain without a known provider may become a curse wrapped in canvas.

Do not name the captain. Do not name the customer. Do not name the final shop where the goods may one day appear beneath flattering light. Name the one who provides the goods.

TASK FOR THE CREW:
What one-word term means a person or company that provides goods or materials to another business?
hints: A business buys from this party before selling onward.
The word begins with S.
stages.wrong_reply: The false merchant smiles wider, which is never a good sign. Ye have named a buyer or seller, perhaps, but not the party that provides goods to another business.
stages.success_reply:
The false merchant loses his smile, then his shadow, and finally his shape, until nothing remains but a faint smell of wet rope and dishonest ink.

Recovered word: SUPPLIER.

The chain now has a face behind the cargo, and the crew may press onward; but clouds gather over the river, and goods that cannot safely move must first find shelter.
stages.next_location: Move toward Pushkinskaya Embankment / river pier area.

stages.address_hint: Pushkinskaya Embankment near river pier area
stages.message:
Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Latitude: 55°43'N | Longitude: 37°35'E
Entry VII: The Stormhold

A black squall gathers over the water, and the river begins to wear the colour of old iron.

No captain with sense sends loaded cargo into such weather. The waves lash the quay, gulls cry like souls denied passage, and even the cursed compass shivers as if it knows that movement at the wrong time is not courage, but waste.

When goods cannot yet move onward, they must wait somewhere secure. Not in the market, where buyers may confuse storage with sale. Not loose upon the dock, where rain, theft and chaos rule. They must be placed under a roof, recorded, protected and held until the next stage of the route can begin.

TASK FOR THE CREW:
What one-word term means a building where goods are stored before they are sold, shipped or distributed?
hints: Goods wait there before the next leg of the journey.
It is not a shop. Customers usually do not browse there.
stages.wrong_reply: The squall grows darker, and the cargo remains exposed on the quay. Ye have named a shop or market, perhaps, but not the place where goods are stored and protected.
stages.success_reply:
The storm pulls back from the quay, and the cargo survives beneath the imagined roof of the Stormhold.

Recovered word: WAREHOUSE.

The goods are safe for now, but cargo locked forever is only treasure buried badly; sooner or later it must move outward toward those who need it.
stages.next_location: Proceed to Golitsyn Pond in Gorky Park.

stages.message:
Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Latitude: 55°43'N | Longitude: 37°36'E
Entry VIII: The Splitting Current

The water here does not command like the river; it circles, reflects and divides, as though the map has been folded into a quiet pond and left for the crew to interpret.

A chest of goods has no true value if it remains in one place forever. Rum locked in the captain’s cabin does not warm a tavern. Silk sealed in a hold does not clothe a merchant’s daughter. Medicine kept in a warehouse does not save the sick.

Trade becomes trade only when goods move outward through channels, routes and decisions until they reach customers, retailers or final destinations. The Splitting Current will drag the cargo apart unless ye name the process that gives movement its purpose.

TASK FOR THE CREW:
What one-word term means the process of moving goods to customers, retailers or final destinations?
hints: It is about getting goods to the places where they are needed.
A product is not useful if it never reaches the customer.
stages.wrong_reply: The current splits the cargo into useless fragments. Ye have named movement, perhaps, but not the organised process of sending goods outward to destinations.
stages.success_reply:
The Splitting Current settles, and the pond gives back the reflection of a route that no longer breaks into useless fragments.

Recovered word: DISTRIBUTION.

The goods are no longer trapped. They move with purpose, though one larger question remains: who commands all these routes, shelters, delays, flows and risks together?
stages.next_location: Walk toward Krymskaya Embankment / Muzeon waterfront.

stages.address_hint: Krymskaya Embankment and Muzeon waterfront area
stages.message:
Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Latitude: 55°44'N | Longitude: 37°36'E
Entry IX: The Flying Dutchman’s Chart

A map unfurls along the waterfront, though no mortal hand holds it, and its edges curl like sails scorched by ghostly fire.

It belongs to the Flying Dutchman, cursed to wander every route and yet arrive nowhere. Upon the chart are marked missed deliveries, broken storage points, wasted voyages, wrong turns, overloaded paths, lost crates, idle crews and customers waiting at harbours where no ship appears.

This is the great trial of the voyage, for no single word already restored is enough. Not inventory alone. Not freight alone. Not procurement, supplier, warehouse, bottleneck or distribution alone.

The crew must name the larger art that plans and manages how goods, storage, transport and information move together through the entire supply chain.

TASK FOR THE CREW:
What one-word term names the planning and management of the movement of goods, storage, transport and information through a supply chain?
hints: It includes transport, storage, timing, route planning and information flow.
The whole quest belongs to this field.
stages.wrong_reply: The Flying Dutchman’s chart twists into impossible routes. Ye have named one part of the system, perhaps, but not the full art of managing movement, storage, transport and information together.
stages.success_reply:
The cursed chart folds itself shut, and the Flying Dutchman’s ink drains from the page like dark water leaving a cracked hull.

Recovered word: LOGISTICS.

Nine locks have opened, and the river now carries the crew toward its final judgement: the stone ship, the admiral’s shadow, and the price every cargo must face when it crosses into trade.
stages.next_location: Go to the Monument to Peter I for the final route trial.

stages.address_hint: Monument to Peter I, Moscow River / Krymskaya Embankment area
stages.message:
Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Latitude: 55°44'N | Longitude: 37°36'E
Entry X: The Admiral’s Toll

At last, crew, the river has delivered us to the stone ship, where mast, sail and command rise above the water as if some admiral from a forgotten age had chosen Moscow itself for his final harbour.

This is no common quay, no tavern corner and no merchant’s stall where a careless captain might bargain his way past trouble with a wink and a pouch of doubloons. Here the route ends before a monument of power, navigation and empire, and the Logbook warns that no cargo may pass its final judgement without answering for the price of crossing borders.

From the mist below the hull, the Customs Dead begin to climb. Their coats are stiff with river salt, their ledgers are chained to their wrists, and their ink, though drowned for centuries, still knows how to write a debt.

Captain Mordecai Flint cheated merchants, governors, admirals and kings, yet even he feared the toll gate, because a false name on a cargo can turn a costly passage into a profitable lie.

TASK FOR THE CREW:
What one-word term means taxes or duties placed on imported or exported goods?
hints: These are trade taxes paid when goods cross borders.
The word begins with T and often appears in discussions of global trade.
stages.wrong_reply: The Customs Dead refuse your coin, and their drowned ledgers open again. Ye have named a cost, perhaps, but not the trade taxes demanded when cargo crosses borders.
stages.success_reply:
The Logbook accepts your answer, and the Customs Dead close their drowned ledgers one by one, as if your word has paid a debt that gold itself could not settle.

Recovered word: TARIFFS.

The tenth lock breaks with the sound of an anchor chain snapping free from the belly of a wreck, and for one breathless moment the river grows so still that even the gulls seem afraid to cross it.

The Cargo of Eternity has surfaced at last, but Captain Flint’s final lesson remains: no treasure can make a pirate great until a choice reveals what sort of soul commands the ship.
stages.next_location: Stay near the Monument to Peter I. The Captain’s Trial begins here.

final.message:
Ship’s Log, April 3rd, Year of Our Lord 1724
Final Entry: The Captain’s Trial

The stone ship towers above the river, and beneath its impossible sails the Logbook opens for the final time.

The route has been restored.

What Captain Mordecai Flint tried to scatter across the river now lies before the crew as one complete map.

Yet no chest of gold rises from the water.

The treasure is stranger than that.

The Logbook reveals the thing Flint valued above all crowns, jewels and Spanish silver: The Cargo of Eternity — a perfect supply chain that could move goods across the world without delay, loss, false suppliers, broken storage, unpaid tariffs, blocked routes or failed delivery.

With such a chain, a captain could command not merely a ship, nor even a fleet, but the movement of trade itself.

Then the air turns cold, and the ghost of Captain Mordecai Flint laughs somewhere above the rigging, though no rigging stands there.

“Treasure,” he whispers, “does not reveal a pirate’s soul. Choice does.”

FINAL QUESTION:
What will ye do with the perfect supply chain?

Send one word:

SHARE
SELL
DESTROY
HIDE

choices.share:
PIRATE PROFILE:
THE CHARTER CAPTAIN

You choose to share the Cargo of Eternity.

Your crew believes trade should serve more than greed. Fair routes, honest suppliers, visible tariffs and responsible procurement become your new flag.

You will never be the fastest pirate on the water, nor the richest scoundrel in the harbour, but merchants will trust your name, workers will bless your routes, and ports will open their gates when your sails appear on the horizon.

Strength: ethics, cooperation and long-term reputation
Risk: slower gains in a world full of faster scoundrels
Final fate: Your flag becomes a promise.
choices.sell:
PIRATE PROFILE:
THE GOLDEN FREEBOOTER

You sell the Cargo of Eternity to the highest bidder.

Doubloons flood your decks, merchant princes beg for your favour, and every port whispers that no captain ever turned a map into money so quickly.

You call it strategy. Others call it greed with a polished hat.

Strength: profit instinct and bold decision-making
Risk: placing too much power in the wrong hands
Final fate: You retire rich, but every storm sounds like a lawsuit.
choices.destroy:
PIRATE PROFILE:
THE STORMBREAKER

You destroy the Cargo of Eternity.

A perfect supply chain, in your eyes, would one day give one captain, one crown or one company too much power over the world’s routes. Better to smash the compass than let the seas become a prison of perfect efficiency.

The Kraken roars beneath the water, and perhaps it approves.

Strength: independence and resistance to control
Risk: chaos, waste and broken trade
Final fate: No one owns the perfect chain. No one can use it either.
choices.hide:
PIRATE PROFILE:
THE GHOST QUARTERMASTER

You hide the Cargo of Eternity.

No auction. No charity. No public glory. Only your crew knows how to move goods faster than storms, quieter than fog and cleaner than any customs ledger suspects.

Your ship is never late. Your suppliers never speak. Your warehouses never lose a crate. Your rivals never understand how you do it.

Strength: control, secrecy and strategic mastery
Risk: paranoia, isolation and moral drift
Final fate: Your name is never spoken twice, but every market feels your shadow.

organizer_note: Students are not looking for words written on signs. They are translating pirate metaphors into supply-chain vocabulary. Every answer is one English word. If they understand the idea but cannot remember the term, they may use HINT.
